title: Multi-volume ballast pool coordinator
description:
## Deliverable
Support ballast files distributed across multiple volumes so each monitored filesystem has its own relief pool. Fixes the fundamental gap where ballast on /var can't help /data.

## The Problem
The current design has a single ballast.location. On machines with multiple data volumes (/data, /scratch, /home), ballast on /var/lib/sbh/ballast can only relieve pressure on the root filesystem. If /data fills up, deleting ballast on /var does nothing. This breaks the ballast feature for the most common production setup.

## The Solution: Per-Volume Ballast Pools

### Architecture
Each monitored filesystem gets its own ballast pool:
- /data has ballast at /data/.sbh/ballast/
- /scratch has ballast at /scratch/.sbh/ballast/
- /home has ballast at /home/.sbh/ballast/
- Root filesystem uses /var/lib/sbh/ballast/ (default)

### Configuration
```toml
[ballast]
auto_provision = true           # Auto-create pools on each monitored mount
per_volume_file_count = 5       # Default files per volume
per_volume_file_size_mb = 1024  # Default size per file

# Override for specific mounts
[ballast.overrides."/data"]
file_count = 10
file_size_mb = 2048             # /data is large, bigger ballast

[ballast.overrides."/scratch"]
file_count = 3
file_size_mb = 512              # /scratch is small

[ballast.overrides."/tmp"]
enabled = false                 # Never ballast tmpfs (defeats purpose)
```

### BallastPoolCoordinator
```rust
pub struct BallastPoolCoordinator {
    pools: HashMap<MountPoint, BallastPool>,
}

pub struct BallastPool {
    mount_point: PathBuf,
    ballast_dir: PathBuf,          // e.g., /data/.sbh/ballast/
    manager: BallastManager,       // existing manager, per-pool instance
    fs_type: String,               // ext4, xfs, btrfs, etc.
}

impl BallastPoolCoordinator {
    /// Provision all pools (auto-detect from watched_paths + overrides)
    pub fn provision_all(&mut self) -> Result<Vec<ProvisionReport>, SbhError>;
    
    /// Release ballast for a SPECIFIC filesystem under pressure
    pub fn release_for_mount(&mut self, mount: &Path, count: usize) -> Result<ReleaseReport, SbhError>;
    
    /// Replenish a specific pool when its filesystem recovers
    pub fn replenish_for_mount(&mut self, mount: &Path) -> Result<ProvisionReport, SbhError>;
    
    /// Get inventory across all pools
    pub fn inventory(&self) -> Vec<PoolInventory>;
    
    /// Total releasable bytes across all pools
    pub fn total_releasable(&self) -> u64;
}
```

### Auto-Detection Logic
On startup:
1. Enumerate all mount points from scanner.watched_paths
2. Resolve each path to its mount point (e.g., /data/projects -> /data)
3. Deduplicate by mount point
4. For each unique mount: check if ballast pool exists, provision if needed
5. Apply overrides from config
6. Skip tmpfs/ramfs mounts (ballast on RAM defeats the purpose)
7. Skip read-only mounts

### Filesystem Type Awareness
Different provisioning strategies per filesystem type:
- ext4/xfs: use fallocate() for instant provisioning
- btrfs/zfs: write random data (CoW prevents fallocate from reserving actual blocks)
- tmpfs: skip entirely (warn in logs)
- NFS: skip by default (warn: network ballast is unreliable)

### Integration with Pressure Response
When PID controller says ReleaseBallast for mount /data:
1. Coordinator routes to /data's pool
2. Releases N files from that specific pool
3. Verifies space freed on /data (not on /var or anywhere else)
4. If /data has no pool (e.g., not provisioned), log warning and skip to artifact scanning

### sbh ballast status (enhanced)
```
Volume    │ Pool Location           │ Files │ Available │ Reclaimable
/data     │ /data/.sbh/ballast      │ 10/10 │ 10        │ 20.0 GB
/scratch  │ /scratch/.sbh/ballast   │ 3/3   │ 3         │ 1.5 GB
/         │ /var/lib/sbh/ballast    │ 5/5   │ 5         │ 5.0 GB
/tmp      │ (skipped: tmpfs)        │  -    │ -         │ -
Total:    │                         │ 18/18 │ 18        │ 26.5 GB
```

## Design Rationale
This is a genuine architectural gap. The single-volume ballast breaks on the most common production setup (separate data and system volumes). Agent swarms often use machines with /data on a large partition and / on a smaller root — ballast on root can't help when /data fills up. Implementation extends BallastManager from a single inventory to a HashMap<MountPoint, BallastPool> — significant but well-contained.

## Acceptance Criteria
- Each monitored filesystem gets its own ballast pool
- Auto-detection correctly identifies mount points from watched_paths
- Per-volume overrides work correctly
- Filesystem type detection selects correct provisioning strategy
- tmpfs and NFS mounts are skipped with warnings
- Release targets the correct volume's pool
- sbh ballast status shows per-volume inventory
- Provisioning respects per-volume free space (don't provision if < 20% free)
- Unit tests: pool coordination, mount point resolution, override merging
- Integration test: two mock volumes -> provision both -> release on specific volume -> verify
- Test: volume with no pool -> graceful fallback to artifact scanning
close_reason: Implemented BallastPoolCoordinator with per-volume pool management, auto-detection, config overrides, targeted release, and 12 comprehensive tests. All 36 ballast tests pass.

comments.author: Dicklesworthstone
comments.text: MINOR: Ballast file index design — each BallastPool (per-filesystem) maintains its OWN index of ballast files (Vec<BallastFile>). The BallastPoolCoordinator holds a HashMap<PathBuf, BallastPool> keyed by mount point. There is NO global ballast file index. This keeps pools independent and avoids cross-pool coordination complexity.

title: Set restrictive permissions on JSONL and notification log files
description: JSONL logger and notification file writer use OpenOptions without explicit mode, inheriting default umask. Apply 0o600 on file creation for consistency with state.json and merkle checkpoint fixes.

title: [macOS E7] Self-monitoring (don't be the problem; respect Energy Impact)
description:
# Epic 7: Self-monitoring on macOS (don't be the problem)

## Why this matters

A disk-pressure helper that itself uses 2% CPU continuously, balloons to 800 MB RSS, and shows up red in Activity Monitor's Energy Impact column will rightly be uninstalled. `src/daemon/self_monitor.rs` (37 KB) tracks own resource use and self-throttles. On Linux it reads `/proc/self/status`. On Mac it needs Mach calls.

## Mac self-monitoring APIs

1. **`mach_task_self()` + `task_info(TASK_BASIC_INFO_64, ...)`** → resident_size (RSS), virtual_size, etc.
2. **`task_info(TASK_THREAD_TIMES_INFO, ...)`** → user_time, system_time across all threads.
3. **`proc_pid_rusage(getpid(), RUSAGE_INFO_V4, ...)`** → I/O totals, idle wakeups (`ri_pkg_idle_wkups`, `ri_interrupt_wkups`). Critical for Energy Impact awareness.
4. **`thread_info`** for per-thread CPU.

## Energy Impact considerations

macOS calculates Energy Impact from: CPU time, idle wakeups, GPU use, network I/O, location use. Background daemons should:
- Use NSURLSession for any network (we have none, so OK).
- Avoid timers under 30s when possible. Our daemon polls every 15s default — borderline. Document tradeoff.
- Use `dispatch_source` instead of polling timers where possible.
- Set `ProcessType=Background` in plist (handles I/O priority + scheduling).
- Mark with `LowPriorityIO=true` in plist.

## Required sbh behavior

- Daemon tracks its own RSS, CPU, idle wakeups continuously.
- If RSS exceeds threshold (default 100 MB), log warning + run smaller scan windows.
- If RSS exceeds hard cap (default 500 MB), self-restart via `launchctl kickstart` (controlled crash).
- Per-tick budget: each daemon tick must complete in < 200 ms. If consistently over, increase tick interval.
- `sbh status` reports own RSS, CPU%, idle_wakeups/sec, last_tick_duration_ms.

## Tasks (see child beads)

1. Mach FFI bindings: `mach_task_self`, `task_info` with `TASK_BASIC_INFO_64`, `TASK_THREAD_TIMES_INFO`, `THREAD_BASIC_INFO`.
2. `SelfStats` struct cross-platform: `rss_bytes`, `vm_bytes`, `cpu_user_us`, `cpu_system_us`, `idle_wakeups`, `bytes_read`, `bytes_written`.
3. Wire to existing daemon self-monitor loop: replace `/proc/self/*` with PAL call.
4. Add `ProcessType=Background` and `LowPriorityIO=true` to launchd plist (linked from E5 plist generation).
5. Energy Impact CI test: run daemon for 60 seconds, capture `taskinfo`, assert idle_wakeups < 100/sec, RSS < 50 MB at idle.
6. Self-restart on RSS exceeded: implement controlled crash → exit nonzero → launchd restarts.
7. Self-throttle: under sustained pressure, increase tick interval from 15s → 30s → 60s adaptively.

## Why this matters specifically for Mac

The operator runs many Electron apps + agents. Activity Monitor is a daily ritual. If sbh shows up red, the operator (and other Mac users) will kill or disable it. We have one shot to be invisible. Linux operators tolerate higher daemon overhead because they're usually on servers; Mac users are on laptops with battery anxiety.

## Failure modes

- RSS measurement off by 10x because we confused units (Mach uses bytes, /proc uses kB-as-strings). Always normalize to bytes internally.
- Self-restart loop: RSS exceeded → exit → launchd restarts → RSS exceeded → ... Mitigation: ThrottleInterval=60 in plist, exponential backoff after 3 restarts.
- Idle wakeups inflated by polling timer at 1s interval → energy red. Use 15s minimum, dispatch_source for events.
close_reason: Completed macOS self-monitoring/energy epic: Mach/libproc self stats, daemon self-monitor wiring, launchd background/low-priority plist settings, energy budget CI, RSS hard-cap restart, and adaptive tick throttling are all closed in child beads bd-wiqg.1 through bd-wiqg.7.

title: [E7.1] Mach FFI bindings (mach_task_self, task_info)
description: Mach FFI bindings: mach_task_self, task_info with TASK_BASIC_INFO_64 and TASK_THREAD_TIMES_INFO and THREAD_BASIC_INFO. Wrappers return RSS bytes, virtual_size, cpu_user_us, cpu_system_us. Refer to mach/task_info.h. Test: assert RSS of current process is plausible (greater than 1MB, less than total RAM).
close_reason: Completed: added an isolated macOS-only sbh_mach crate for the unavoidable unsafe Mach task_info/thread_info boundary, exposed safe current_mach_task_usage/current_mach_thread_basic_info adapters from src/platform/macos/sys.rs, and added live macOS plausibility tests for RSS and thread run state. Proof: cargo fmt --check; git diff --check; CARGO_TARGET_DIR=/tmp/sbh-wiqg1-macos cargo test --no-default-features --features cli,daemon,sqlite --lib current_mach -- --nocapture; CARGO_TARGET_DIR=/tmp/sbh-wiqg1-macos cargo clippy --no-default-features --features cli,daemon,sqlite --lib -- -D warnings; rch exec -- env CARGO_TARGET_DIR=/tmp/sbh-wiqg1-check cargo check --all-targets; rch exec -- env CARGO_TARGET_DIR=/tmp/sbh-wiqg1-clippy cargo clippy --all-targets -- -D warnings.

title: [E7.2] Cross-platform SelfStats struct + Mac impl
description: SelfStats struct cross-platform: rss_bytes, vm_bytes, cpu_user_us, cpu_system_us, idle_wakeups, bytes_read, bytes_written. Mac populated via E7.1 + proc_pid_rusage(getpid()). Linux uses /proc/self/status + /proc/self/io.
close_reason: Implemented cross-platform SelfStats: Linux /proc reader plus macOS Mach/libproc PAL stats; verified with macOS focused test/clippy and Linux rch check/clippy/tests.

title: [E7.3] Wire daemon self-monitor through pal.self_stats()
description: Wire to existing daemon self-monitor loop in src/daemon/self_monitor.rs: replace /proc/self/* reads with pal.self_stats() call. Existing thresholds and self-throttle behavior unchanged. Linux behavior identical (refactor only).
close_reason: Wired daemon self-monitor and summary RSS reporting through Platform::self_stats(); preserved existing RSS threshold behavior; verified macOS focused test/clippy plus Linux rch focused test/check/clippy.

title: [E7.4] Plist ProcessType=Background + LowPriorityIO
description: Add ProcessType=Background and LowPriorityIO=true to launchd plist. Linked from E5 plist generation; this task verifies the keys are present in the generated plist and that they have the expected effect (Activity Monitor shows sbh in Background category, low priority I/O scheduling).
close_reason: Verified launchd plist sets ProcessType=Background and LowPriorityIO=true with parsed plist regression coverage.

title: [E7.5] Energy Impact CI test
description: Energy Impact CI test: run daemon for 60 seconds in foreground on macOS runner, capture taskinfo via proc_pid_rusage, assert idle_wakeups less than 100 per second on average, RSS less than 50 MB at idle. Skip when running on slow CI runner with marker.
close_reason: Added macOS idle energy impact integration test and CI workflow step for release-binary RSS/wakeup budgets.

title: [E7.6] Self-restart on RSS exceeded (controlled crash)
description: Self-restart on RSS exceeded: implement controlled crash. When RSS greater than configured hard cap (default 500 MB), log fatal, persist state, exit nonzero. launchd KeepAlive=Crashed=true triggers restart. ThrottleInterval=60 prevents tight restart loop.
close_reason: Implemented configurable daemon RSS hard cap with fatal state persistence and nonzero controlled exit; added real daemon integration coverage.

title: [E7.7] Adaptive tick interval throttle under sustained pressure
description: Self-throttle: under sustained pressure (RSS greater than soft cap or daemon tick consistently more than 200 ms), increase tick interval from 15s to 30s to 60s adaptively. Reset on pressure clear.
close_reason: Implemented adaptive daemon tick throttle with 30s/60s backoff under sustained RSS or slow-tick pressure, plus focused unit coverage.

title: EWMA disk usage rate estimator
description:
## Deliverable
Exponentially Weighted Moving Average estimator that tracks the RATE of disk consumption, enabling predictive intervention before storage actually runs out.

## Technical Approach
### Theory (Alien Graveyard: EWMA from time-series analysis)
EWMA smooths noisy measurements while giving more weight to recent observations:
  EWMA_t = α × x_t + (1 - α) × EWMA_{t-1}
where α ∈ (0, 1) is the smoothing factor (higher = more reactive).

For sbh, we track:
- bytes_consumed_per_second: how fast disk is being consumed
- time_to_exhaustion: estimated seconds until disk is full at current rate
- acceleration: is the consumption rate itself increasing? (second derivative)

### Implementation
```rust
pub struct DiskRateEstimator {
    alpha: f64,                     // smoothing factor (default 0.3)
    ewma_bytes_per_sec: f64,        // smoothed consumption rate
    ewma_acceleration: f64,         // smoothed rate of rate change
    last_free_bytes: u64,
    last_sample_time: Instant,
    samples_count: u64,
    min_samples_for_prediction: u64, // need at least N samples (default 3)
}

pub struct RateEstimate {
    pub bytes_per_second: f64,       // current smoothed rate (positive = consuming)
    pub seconds_to_exhaustion: f64,  // predicted time until 0% free
    pub seconds_to_threshold: f64,   // predicted time until red threshold
    pub acceleration: f64,           // rate change (positive = accelerating)
    pub confidence: f64,             // 0.0-1.0 based on sample count and variance
    pub trend: Trend,
}

pub enum Trend {
    Stable,        // consumption rate is constant
    Accelerating,  // consumption rate is increasing (agent swarm ramping up)
    Decelerating,  // consumption rate is decreasing (builds finishing)
    Recovering,    // disk is being freed (negative consumption rate)
}
```

### Prediction
time_to_exhaustion = free_bytes / bytes_per_second (when rate > 0)
With acceleration correction: t = (-v + sqrt(v² + 2*a*d)) / a (quadratic model)
where v = current rate, a = acceleration, d = free bytes remaining

### Adaptive Alpha
When disk consumption is very bursty (e.g., cargo build starts), increase alpha temporarily to be more responsive. When stable, decrease alpha for smoother estimates.

### Why This Matters
Without rate estimation, sbh can only react AFTER thresholds are crossed. With EWMA prediction, sbh can start preparing BEFORE the threshold is crossed:
- If time_to_exhaustion < 5 minutes → start preemptive cleanup
- If acceleration is positive → start monitoring more frequently
- If trend is Recovering → reduce scan frequency to save CPU

## Design Rationale
EWMA is the right tool because: (1) disk consumption is noisy (builds start/stop), (2) we need recency-biased smoothing, (3) it's O(1) memory and O(1) per update, (4) the alpha parameter lets us tune reactivity. The acceleration term (second derivative) catches the "10 agents started compiling simultaneously" scenario before it fills the disk.

## Acceptance Criteria
- EWMA correctly smooths noisy input sequences
- Predictions are reasonable for linear consumption patterns
- Quadratic prediction handles acceleration correctly
- Confidence increases with sample count
- Trend detection correctly identifies all four states
- Thread-safe (the monitoring loop updates this from a single thread, but readers may be concurrent)
- Unit tests with synthetic consumption patterns
acceptance_criteria: 1. Unit tests validate EWMA smoothing, acceleration estimates, and confidence computation across synthetic traces. 2. Integration tests verify estimator outputs feed controller thresholds without instability. 3. E2E pressure timelines validate early-warning trigger quality against known workloads. 4. Estimator remains deterministic for fixed sample streams and alpha settings. 5. Detailed estimator logs include raw samples, EWMA value, derivative estimates, confidence, and trend state.
close_reason: EWMA rate estimator implemented in monitor/ewma.rs (255 lines): DiskRateEstimator with adaptive alpha, EWMA rate/acceleration/confidence, Trend classification (Stable/Accelerating/Decelerating/Recovering), quadratic projection with linear fallback, unit tests.

title: Multi-factor deterministic candidacy scoring engine
description:
The scoring engine that computes a file_deletion_candidacy_score for every discovered artifact, combining multiple independent factors into a single deterministic score with hard safety vetoes.

## CRITICAL CONTRACT: Pure Computation, Zero I/O
The scoring engine is a PURE FUNCTION. Given identical inputs, it produces identical outputs. It NEVER performs filesystem I/O, network calls, or any side effects. All data it needs is provided by callers:
- Path + metadata → from DirectoryWalker (bd-1w9)
- Artifact classification → from PatternRegistry (bd-1sw)
- pressure_multiplier → from PID controller output (bd-3po), passed in by main loop
- Structural markers → pre-collected by walker as part of WalkEntry.structural_markers: Vec<String>

This means the walker must collect structural markers (checking for subdirs like incremental/, deps/, .fingerprint/) during traversal, and attach them to WalkEntry. The scoring engine then uses these pre-collected markers without touching the filesystem.

## Scoring Formula (Alien Artifact: information-theoretic multi-factor scoring)

```
candidacy_score = pressure_multiplier × Σ(weight_i × factor_i)
```

With hard vetoes that force score to 0.0 regardless of other factors.

## Factor Functions (each normalized to [0.0, 1.0])

**f_location(path)**: Location safety factor
```
/tmp, /data/tmp, /dev/shm     → 0.95  (temp dirs, very safe)
/data/projects/*/target        → 0.80  (build output, safe)
/data/projects/*/.target*      → 0.85  (hidden build output, safe)
/data/projects/*/.tmp_*        → 0.90  (explicitly temporary)
~/.cache/*                     → 0.60  (caches, usually safe)
/home/*/projects/*             → 0.40  (user projects, moderate risk)
/home/*/Documents/*            → 0.10  (user data, high risk)
/ system directories           → 0.00  (never touch)
```

**f_name(entry, classification)**: Name/type classification factor
```
classification.combined_confidence directly (0.0-1.0)
Boost +0.1 if name contains "tmp", "temp", "cache"
Boost +0.15 if classification category is RustTarget
Penalty -0.3 if name contains "backup", "save", "important"
```

**f_age(modified_time, now)**: Age sweet-spot factor (bell curve, not linear)
Note: "now" is passed explicitly for determinism (never call SystemTime::now())
```
< 30 minutes      → 0.00  (too recent, may be in active use)
30 min - 2 hours   → 0.20  (probably still in use)
2 - 4 hours        → 0.70  (cooling off)
4 - 10 hours       → 1.00  (sweet spot: old enough to be stale)
10 - 24 hours      → 0.85  (definitely stale)
1 - 7 days         → 0.60  (old, but user might remember it)
7 - 30 days        → 0.40  (very old, ambiguous)
> 30 days          → 0.25  (ancient - might be intentional)
```

**f_size(bytes)**: Size-efficiency factor (prefer deleting large items)
```
< 1 MB             → 0.05  (not worth the syscall overhead)
1 - 10 MB          → 0.20
10 - 100 MB        → 0.40
100 MB - 1 GB      → 0.70
1 - 10 GB          → 1.00  (maximum value - huge space recovery)
10 - 50 GB         → 0.90  (enormous, slight extra caution)
> 50 GB            → 0.75  (suspiciously large, extra caution)
```

**f_structure(structural_markers)**: Internal structure indicators
Uses pre-collected structural_markers from WalkEntry (NO filesystem I/O here):
```
Contains .fingerprint/ or incremental/  → 0.95  (definitely build artifacts)
Contains deps/ + build/                 → 0.85  (very likely build artifacts)
Contains .git/                          → 0.00  (VETO: this is a repo!)
Contains Cargo.toml at root             → 0.05  (this is a project, not a target)
Contains only .d, .o, .rlib files       → 0.90  (build outputs)
```

## Weights
Default weights (configurable):
```
w_location  = 0.25
w_name      = 0.25
w_age       = 0.20
w_size      = 0.15
w_structure = 0.15
```

## Pressure Multiplier
From PID controller urgency (0.0-1.0), passed in by caller:
```
urgency 0.0    → multiplier 1.0  (normal scoring)
urgency 0.3    → multiplier 1.3  (slightly lower bar)
urgency 0.5    → multiplier 1.5  (moderate pressure)
urgency 0.8    → multiplier 2.0  (aggressive)
urgency 1.0    → multiplier 3.0  (emergency - almost anything goes)
```

## Hard Vetoes (override score to 0.0)
These CANNOT be overridden even under maximum pressure:
1. structural_markers contains ".git" → VETO (never delete repos)
2. Path matches system directory pattern → VETO
3. File age < min_file_age_minutes (default 30) → VETO (age computed from passed-in "now")
4. Path matches any user-configured exclusion → VETO
5. File is currently open by a process → VETO (this is the ONE check that requires I/O — but it's done by the CALLER before invoking the scoring engine, and passed as an `is_open: bool` flag)

## Implementation
```rust
pub struct ScoringEngine {
    weights: ScoringWeights,
    pattern_registry: ArtifactPatternRegistry,
    excluded_paths: Vec<PathBuf>,
    min_file_age: Duration,
}

/// All inputs needed to score a single candidate. No I/O needed.
pub struct ScoringInput {
    pub path: PathBuf,
    pub size_bytes: u64,
    pub modified_at: SystemTime,
    pub structural_markers: Vec<String>,  // pre-collected by walker
    pub is_open: bool,                     // pre-checked by caller
    pub now: SystemTime,                   // explicit timestamp for determinism
    pub pressure_urgency: f64,             // from PID controller
}

pub struct CandidacyScore {
    pub path: PathBuf,
    pub total_score: f64,
    pub factors: ScoreFactors,
    pub vetoed: bool,
    pub veto_reason: Option<String>,
    pub classification: ArtifactClassification,
    pub size_bytes: u64,
    pub age: Duration,
}
```

## Determinism
Given the same ScoringInput, the scoring engine MUST produce identical CandidacyScore. No randomness, no implicit time-dependency (all time-dependent computations use the passed-in `now` field). This enables testing and reproducibility.


## Acceptance Criteria
- Deterministic scoring and ordering for identical inputs
- Hard vetoes always dominate computed score
- Pressure scaling monotonic with urgency
- Boundary-value tests for all factors
- Pure-computation contract (no I/O in scorer)
- Integration ranking test with realistic fixtures
acceptance_criteria: 1. Deterministic scoring: identical ScoringInput yields identical CandidacyScore and ordering. 2. Hard vetoes always override score for git/system/excluded/recent/open-file paths. 3. Pressure multiplier mapping is monotonic in urgency. 4. Factor boundary values are unit-tested. 5. Pure-function contract holds: no filesystem or network I/O inside scoring engine. 6. Explainability fields are always populated for decision traces. 7. Integration test validates realistic ranking and veto behavior.
close_reason: Implemented in scanner/scoring.rs

comments.author: Dicklesworthstone
comments.text: REVIEW: Removed dependency on bd-2pj (FsStatsCollector). The scoring engine is a PURE COMPUTATION — it takes pre-collected metadata (from walker) and a pressure_multiplier (from PID controller) as inputs. It should never call filesystem APIs directly. This was an erroneous dependency that added unnecessary critical path length. The pressure_multiplier the scoring engine needs comes from the PID controller's output, which the main loop passes in when invoking scoring.

comments.author: Dicklesworthstone
comments.text: REVIEW-2: Removed bd-1w9 dependency. The scoring engine is pure computation with zero I/O. It takes ScoringInput struct populated by the caller. The walker PROVIDES data but the scorer does not import walker code. Data contract: walker produces WalkEntry with structural_markers, caller constructs ScoringInput adding pressure_urgency + is_open + now timestamp.

title: [macOS E14] Testing + CI (macos runners, snapshot tests, integration)
description:
# Epic 14: Testing & CI for macOS

## Why thorough testing is non-negotiable here

Mac port introduces dozens of FFI calls, unsafe Mach syscalls, plist generation, launchctl shelling. Every one is a potential silent failure mode. Without comprehensive tests, "works on my machine" becomes "broken on operator's machine" with no warning.

## Test layers required

1. **Unit tests on Mac syscall wrappers.** PAL methods called individually: assert returned data has expected shape, units, ranges.
2. **Integration tests on launchd.** Install LaunchAgent, query via launchctl, kickstart, bootout — in CI on macos-latest runner.
3. **Snapshot tests on plist generation.** Generated XML matches frozen golden files. Canonicalize before comparison (sort keys, normalize whitespace).
4. **Property tests on cleanup heuristics.** Generate random dir trees, assert sacred paths never touched, assert dir contents drive confidence correctly.
5. **End-to-end tests.** Spin up disk-pressure scenario (create N GB ballast, fill remaining), run scan + clean, assert recovery, restore baseline.
6. **Regression tests.** Each bug we fix gets a test case named after the issue.
7. **Bench tests on hot paths.** Daemon poll tick must complete in <200 ms; PAL syscalls < 5 ms each on average.

## CI matrix expansion

GitHub Actions:
- `ubuntu-latest` (existing — Linux x86_64)
- `ubuntu-latest-arm64` (existing or new — Linux arm64)
- `macos-latest` (Apple Silicon — new)
- `macos-13` (Intel — for at least one matrix entry — keep until x86_64 Mac demand drops)

For each: cargo test (unit + integration), cargo build (release binary smoke check), and selected E2E tests.

## Tasks (see child beads)

1. Add `macos-latest` and `macos-13` to GitHub Actions test matrix.
2. CI step: install required Mac tools (xcode-select --install if missing — usually present on runners).
3. CI step: codesign verify on built binary (using ad-hoc signing in CI; real Developer ID signing happens in release workflow only).
4. Test fixture infrastructure: helper to create temp APFS-aware tree mimicking real Mac dir structure (Library/Caches, Library/Developer/Xcode/DerivedData, etc.). Use temp dir with synthetic markers.
5. Snapshot test crate setup (`insta`) for plist outputs.
6. Property test crate setup (`proptest`) for cleanup confidence engine.
7. Integration test: launchd lifecycle (bootstrap → kickstart → bootout in single-user runner).
8. Coverage tracking: add `cargo-llvm-cov` step; report coverage delta per PR; fail if Mac-side coverage drops.
9. CI fail-fast on UnsupportedPlatform errors (those should never appear in green CI).
10. Document in CONTRIBUTING.md: how to run Mac tests locally; how to run Linux tests on a Mac dev machine (cross-compile + run in a Linux VM via lima/utm).

## Test philosophy specific to Mac

- **Don't mock libc/Mach unless absolutely necessary.** Run real syscalls in tests; the temp dir / /tmp / fork-and-exec patterns work fine in CI runners. Mocking syscalls hides FFI bugs.
- **DO mock launchctl in unit tests** (it's a system service we don't want to actually install). Integration tests can install for real with `Label = com.dicklesworthstone.sbh.test.<random>` to avoid colliding with real installs.
- **Avoid time-based flakiness.** No `sleep(60)`-based "wait for daemon to start" — use polling with timeout.
- **Snapshot tests are golden, not floor.** Updating snapshots requires explicit `cargo insta accept`.

## Failure modes

- Skipping CI on Mac when budget tight → regressions land.
- macOS runners are slow (10+ min for full test suite). Mitigation: parallelize, skip slow E2E tests on PR runs, run them on nightly cron.
- launchctl in CI runner has weird state from previous test run. Mitigation: aggressive bootout in test setup; use unique per-test labels.

title: Massive TUI overhaul: FrankentUI-inspired SBH operator cockpit with zero feature regression
description:
Mission
Replace the current dashboard path with a significantly more capable, operator-grade TUI inspired by /dp/frankentui showcase patterns while preserving all existing SBH safety and observability behavior.

Context
- Current implementation is split between cli_app live status rendering and a separate crossterm dashboard module, creating drift and limited UX depth.
- SBH is safety-critical: dashboard behavior must respect daemon state-file contracts, stale-state detection, guarded fallback behavior, and no JSON live streaming for dashboard mode.
- We need an intentional architecture (model/update/view + bounded subscriptions), not ad hoc render loops.

Non-negotiables
- Do not lose any existing dashboard/status information surface (pressure, EWMA/trend visibility, ballast inventory/counters, daemon freshness indicators).
- Preserve existing safety semantics and read-only boundaries unless explicitly adding guarded operator actions.
- Keep deterministic behavior, robust terminal cleanup, and strong test/e2e coverage with detailed logs.

## Success Criteria
- New TUI is the default dashboard path with feature parity plus meaningful operator upgrades from frankentui patterns.
- Full unit/integration/e2e/perf test matrix passes and logs are actionable for failures.
- Documentation is updated so future maintainers can evolve the cockpit without re-deriving intent from old notes.
- Rollback controls and fallback behavior are validated before go-live signoff.

comments.author: Dicklesworthstone
comments.text:
Investigation summary (for future maintainers)

Current dashboard reality:
- `Command::Dashboard` in `src/cli_app.rs` routes to the live status loop (`run_live_status_loop`) with a refresh floor and explicit rejection of live JSON mode.
- A separate `src/cli/dashboard.rs` crossterm implementation exists with richer frame rendering but is not the canonical path today.
- Data contract is anchored on `SelfMonitor::read_state()` of daemon `state.json` (with stale-state threshold) plus filesystem fallback.

Overhaul intent:
- Converge to one canonical dashboard runtime path.
- Keep all existing safety semantics and operator-critical data surfaces.
- Upgrade UX by borrowing high-value patterns from `/dp/frankentui` demos (overview layout, timeline, explainability, command palette, diagnostics) without importing unnecessary demo complexity.

Key guardrails:
- No feature loss.
- No silent degradation of safety or explainability signals.
- Verification track is mandatory before default cutover.

comments.author: Dicklesworthstone
comments.text:
Graph refinement note (post-bv diagnostics)

Used `bv --robot-triage` and `bv --robot-insights` to validate dependency health.

Adjustments made:
- Removed unnecessary track-level block edges that created an articulation bottleneck and over-serialized execution.
- Kept sequencing control at task-level dependencies (more parallelizable while preserving correctness gates).
- Added explicit tasks/deps for licensing+toolchain compliance, schema-shielding, scenario e2e drills, and legacy deprecation policy.

Current graph state:
- No dependency cycles.
- No xzt articulation points.
- Critical bottlenecks now reflect expected high-impact tasks, not accidental graph structure.

comments.author: Dicklesworthstone
comments.text:
Second-pass optimization sweep completed (plan-space hardening)

What was revised:
- Added explicit success-criteria sections to all xzt epics.
- Added deep test beads: PTY interaction harness, deterministic replay suite, property-based invariant suite, structured artifact schema, and legacy-vs-new parity harness.
- Added user-outcome bead: operator workflow benchmark validation feeding UX tuning.
- Strengthened dependency graph so quality-gate and signoff tasks require these new safeguards.

Why this improves user outcomes:
- Stronger guarantee of zero feature loss and safer rollout behavior.
- Better failure diagnosability via standardized artifacts and trace IDs.
- Measurable operator effectiveness improvements, not just visual upgrades.

Graph health after revision:
- `bv` cycle check: none
- `bv` xzt articulation points: none
- Critical bottlenecks are now meaningful implementation gates, not accidental graph structure.

Added explicit pathing runtime->preferences model (bd-xzt.2.10)->preference-driven UX (bd-xzt.3.11)->verification/docs/signoff. Added failure-injection gate (bd-xzt.4.14) into quality-runbook and testing-doc flow. Validation snapshot: br dep cycles=0, br lint=0, bv shows no xzt articulation points. Intentional tradeoff: fewer immediately actionable nodes in exchange for guaranteed test/logging completeness and lower risk of feature regression.

title: Track A: Architecture contracts and migration strategy for FrankentUI adoption
description:
Purpose
Lock down architecture, compatibility constraints, and migration approach before writing runtime code, so we avoid rework and preserve safety-critical semantics.

Scope
- Verify live dashboard/status contracts currently depended on by operators and tests.
- Evaluate frankentui adoption options against SBH stable toolchain and maintenance goals.
- Produce explicit acceptance criteria and failure budgets for the overhaul.

Why this matters
A dashboard rewrite can easily regress hidden operational assumptions (refresh floor behavior, stale-state detection, fallback semantics). This track makes those assumptions explicit and testable.

## Success Criteria
- A signed-off architectural baseline exists (contracts + ADR + acceptance budgets).
- Compliance/toolchain decisions are explicit enough to prevent accidental nightly lock-in or attribution errors.
- Downstream runtime/screen/testing tasks can proceed without architecture ambiguity.
close_reason: Completed: all Track A child beads (bd-xzt.1.1, .1.2, .1.3, .1.4, .1.5, .1.6) are closed with architecture/compliance/IA/gate artifacts delivered

comments.author: Dicklesworthstone
comments.text:
Rationale notes

`/dp/frankentui` is MIT-licensed and offers strong reusable UX patterns, but its broader stack currently carries nightly/toolchain and dependency-surface considerations. This track exists to prevent premature architectural lock-in.

Do not start broad runtime coding before:
1) baseline contracts are frozen,
2) integration strategy ADR is approved,
3) acceptance/perf/error budgets are explicit.

Skipping this sequencing historically causes expensive rewrites and hidden operator regressions.

title: Audit and freeze current dashboard/status behavior contracts (pre-overhaul baseline)
description:
Background
Current behavior is split across `src/cli_app.rs` and `src/cli/dashboard.rs`; parity can be lost unless contracts are codified first.

Work
- Enumerate all user-visible dashboard/status semantics: refresh floor, key bindings, no-JSON live dashboard behavior, stale daemon-state handling, degraded fallback to live fs stats, ballast/counter displays.
- Capture exact data dependencies and schema assumptions (`DaemonState`, `PressureState`, `BallastState`, `Counters`).
- Capture terminal lifecycle invariants (raw mode, alt-screen/cleanup obligations).

Acceptance criteria
- A baseline contract checklist exists and is referenced by all implementation/testing tasks.
- Every baseline item has a planned verification method (unit/integration/e2e/manual).
- No ambiguous “nice to have” items remain in the contract list.
close_reason: Completed: froze dashboard/status baseline contract in docs/dashboard-status-contract-baseline.md and linked from README/docs/testing-and-logging

title: Triage FrankentUI showcase elements and map to SBH-safe reusable components
description:
Background
FrankenTUI has strong demo patterns, but SBH must avoid accidental dependency/toolchain blowups and demo-only complexity.

Work
- Evaluate candidate modules/screens from `/dp/frankentui` for direct reuse vs adaptation vs re-implementation.
- Document dependency and toolchain impact (nightly requirement, crate fan-out, feature gates, licensing obligations).
- Produce a ranked shortlist of “must copy” UX elements (overview layout, timeline, explainability panes, command palette, async task visibility, accessibility toggles).

Acceptance criteria
- Each candidate has a clear disposition (adopt/adapt/reject) with rationale.
- Risks and mitigations are explicit (stability, maintenance, performance, compatibility).
- Result is actionable enough to drive an implementation ADR without re-research.
close_reason: Completed: FrankentUI triage matrix produced at docs/frankentui-triage-matrix.md with full disposition analysis (7 MUST COPY, 4 SHOULD ADAPT, 30 REJECT), dependency/toolchain impact analysis, risk matrix, and ranked shortlist of 12 must-copy UX elements.

title: Choose integration strategy (vendor vs selective adaptation) and publish implementation ADR
description:
Background
The biggest strategic risk is choosing an integration path that blocks delivery or creates long-term maintenance debt.

Work
- Compare at least two viable strategies: (A) broader ftui runtime adoption, (B) selective component adaptation with SBH-native runtime, (C) hybrid if justified.
- Decide feature-flag and migration boundaries, including fallback path expectations.
- Record ownership boundaries and update impact on existing modules (`cli_app`, `cli/dashboard`, telemetry consumers).

Acceptance criteria
- One strategy is explicitly selected with rejection reasons for alternatives.
- ADR includes implementation phases, risk controls, and rollback mechanics.
- All downstream tasks reference this ADR as the source of architectural truth.
close_reason: Completed: Integration strategy ADR published at docs/adr-tui-integration-strategy.md. Decision: selective adaptation. Incorporates baseline contract (bd-xzt.1.1), triage matrix (bd-xzt.1.2), and compliance plan (bd-xzt.1.6). Unblocks bd-xzt.2.1, bd-xzt.2.4, bd-xzt.1.4.

comments.author: Dicklesworthstone
comments.text: Execution start (CalmCompass): picked by bv robot triage as highest-impact actionable blocker (unblocks bd-xzt.1.4 and then bd-xzt.2.1 chain). Implementing the integration-strategy ADR now with explicit alternatives, risk controls, migration phases, and rollback mechanics.

comments.author: Dicklesworthstone
comments.text: Implemented bd-xzt.1.3 deliverable in docs/adr-tui-integration-strategy.md: added mandatory decision invariants, integrated new preference and failure-injection tracks (bd-xzt.2.10, bd-xzt.3.11, bd-xzt.4.14), and added downstream bead mapping table for execution traceability.

title: Design dashboard information architecture and cross-screen navigation map
description:
Background
The new cockpit must optimize operator decision speed under pressure, not just visual polish.

Work
- Define primary/secondary operator journeys (pressure triage, explainability drill-down, cleanup candidate review, ballast response).
- Specify screen topology, pane priorities, and cross-screen navigation model.
- Define when information is always-on vs drill-down (to avoid clutter and missed alerts).

Acceptance criteria
- Navigation and screen IA are concrete enough to implement without UI guesswork.
- Every major SBH workflow is mapped to a clear screen path.
- Layout decisions include rationale tied to operator cognition and incident response speed.

comments.author: Dicklesworthstone
comments.text: Validation pass (CalmCompass):  now provides explicit primary/secondary workflows, screen topology, cross-screen navigation contract, always-on vs drill-down hierarchy, pane priorities, and downstream guardrails. This satisfies bd-xzt.1.4 acceptance criteria and unblocks bd-xzt.1.5/bd-xzt.2.5.

title: Define non-regression acceptance gates and performance/error budgets for TUI rollout
description:
Background
We need objective gates to prevent “looks good” from masking regressions in safety-critical behavior.

Work
- Define parity checklist gates for legacy behavior retention.
- Define dashboard performance targets (refresh cadence, CPU envelope, frame-time jitter tolerance, memory growth limits).
- Define error budget and fallback rules (stale state handling, data source failure behavior, terminal cleanup guarantees).

Acceptance criteria
- Gate list is machine-verifiable where possible and linked to concrete tests.
- Performance/error budgets are explicit numeric targets, not qualitative claims.
- Rollout and rollback tasks consume these gates directly.

comments.author: Dicklesworthstone
comments.text: Completed acceptance-gate specification draft: added docs/tui-acceptance-gates-and-budgets.md with hard/soft gate policy, C-01..C-18 non-regression mapping, explicit performance + error budgets, required rch command sequence, artifact schema, and release decision criteria. Also linked new doc in README Operator Docs and docs/testing-and-logging.md.

title: Establish licensing and toolchain compliance plan for FrankentUI code reuse
description:
Background
Reusing frankentui elements requires explicit compliance decisions (MIT attribution, dependency/toolchain policy).

Work
- Define how attribution/license notices are preserved for copied/adapted code.
- Define acceptable dependency/toolchain posture for SBH (stable-first strategy, feature gates if nightly-only code is unavoidable).
- Document review checklist for future imports from frankentui to prevent silent policy drift.

Acceptance criteria
- Compliance plan is explicit and referenced by implementation PRs.
- Toolchain policy prevents accidental nightly lock-in unless intentionally approved.
- Future self can safely import additional components without legal/toolchain ambiguity.
close_reason: Completed: compliance plan at docs/frankentui-compliance-plan.md

title: Track B: Core TUI runtime, data adapters, and command wiring
description:
Purpose
Implement the new dashboard runtime spine (state model, update loop, subscriptions, renderer integration, data adapters) and integrate it into CLI command flow.

Scope
- Stable command entrypoint and lifecycle management (raw mode, alt screen/inline behavior, cleanup).
- Strongly typed adapters for daemon state, live fs fallback, and telemetry-backed panes.
- Deterministic refresh scheduling and event handling compatible with SBH guardrails.

Why this matters
Without a robust runtime/data spine, advanced screens become brittle and difficult to test.

## Success Criteria
- Dashboard command uses one canonical runtime path with preserved flag semantics and safety behavior.
- Runtime state transitions and async effects are deterministic, bounded, and testable.
- Terminal lifecycle, data adapters, and schema shielding hold under degraded and failure conditions.

comments.author: Dicklesworthstone
comments.text:
Implementation intent

This track should treat runtime/data boundaries as first-class API surfaces:
- command entrypoint,
- model/update transitions,
- adapters for state/log data,
- scheduler/backpressure behavior,
- terminal lifecycle guarantees.

Future self warning:
If these boundaries are blurry, every screen addition will re-open core stability bugs. Keep the spine minimal, deterministic, and well-tested before layering more UI complexity.

title: Create new TUI module scaffold and route sbh dashboard through a single canonical runtime entrypoint
description:
Background
Current dashboard behavior is split; we need one canonical path before richer features.

Work
- Introduce/normalize module layout for the new runtime (model, update, view, adapters, widgets, input, telemetry hooks).
- Wire CLI command dispatch so dashboard invocations enter the new runtime entrypoint (feature-gated if needed during migration).
- Preserve baseline CLI argument semantics (`--refresh-ms` normalization, no live JSON dashboard mode).

Acceptance criteria
- There is exactly one canonical runtime entrypoint for dashboard mode.
- Existing dashboard command flags continue to parse and enforce baseline constraints.
- Legacy code path remains callable only via explicit migration controls if required.
close_reason: Canonical dashboard runtime entrypoint + module scaffold implemented and wired; migration controls + non-regression flag semantics preserved

Preserved existing dashboard constraints ( minimum floor and live JSON rejection). Added focused tests for runtime selection and flag conflicts plus TUI module unit coverage.

comments.author: Dicklesworthstone
comments.text: Completion details: implemented TUI scaffold seams in src/tui (adapters, input, model, render, runtime, telemetry, update, widgets), introduced canonical runtime entrypoint in src/tui/runtime.rs, and routed sbh dashboard dispatch in src/cli_app.rs through one runtime selector with explicit migration controls (--new-dashboard and --legacy-dashboard). Existing contracts were preserved: refresh floor normalization and dashboard live JSON rejection. Added tests for runtime-selection behavior and dashboard flag conflicts, and executed focused TUI unit tests under --features tui.

title: Add dashboard user-preferences model and safe persistence boundaries
description:
## Background
Operators repeatedly reconfigure dashboard defaults (start screen, density, hint verbosity), which adds friction during incidents. Preferences are useful only if they are deterministic, resilient to corruption, and never coupled to safety-critical code paths.

## Goals
- Introduce a versioned preferences schema for dashboard UX settings (default screen, density mode, hint/help verbosity, visual preference toggles).
- Define deterministic merge order: compiled defaults -> persisted preferences -> explicit session/CLI overrides.
- Ensure persistence and read failures cannot block dashboard startup, redraw, navigation, cleanup, or emergency actions.

## Implementation Scope
- Add typed preferences model with strict validation and forward-compatible unknown-field handling.
- Add atomic persistence semantics (temp write + fsync + rename) and debounce policy for write frequency.
- Add bounded error handling path for corrupt/missing/permission-denied preference files.
- Emit explicit in-memory fallback mode when persistence is unavailable (read-only/degraded profile mode).

## Test + Logging Requirements
- Unit tests: schema validation, default merge precedence, corruption fallback, migration/version handling.
- Integration tests: permission failures, partial writes, stale/corrupt preference payloads, startup recovery path.
- E2E hooks: preference reset/reload path captured in scripted dashboard scenarios with per-case trace identifiers.
- Logging: structured preference load/save events with reason codes (applied_default, recovered_corrupt, write_degraded, write_skipped).

## Acceptance Criteria
- Preference corruption/missing data always falls back to deterministic defaults with no panic.
- Preference storage failures are operator-visible but non-blocking for safety-critical workflows.
- Preference behavior is fully covered by unit + integration tests and traceable in e2e logs.

comments.author: Dicklesworthstone
comments.text: Dependency rationale: bd-xzt.2.10 depends on bd-xzt.2.1, bd-xzt.2.2, and bd-xzt.2.5 so preference persistence is anchored to canonical runtime entrypoint, deterministic model semantics, and theme/density primitives before UX integration work begins.

title: Implement deterministic dashboard state model and update loop (Elm-style model/update/cmd)
description:
Background
A deterministic reducer-style state model is needed for reliability, testing, and replayability under pressure.

Work
- Define typed model state for global UI + screen-local domains.
- Implement pure update/reducer pathways for input events, timer ticks, data refreshes, and error events.
- Define bounded command/effect interfaces for async fetches and background work.

Acceptance criteria
- State transitions for the same input sequence are deterministic.
- Reducer/update logic is testable without terminal rendering side effects.
- Async effects are bounded and observable (no untracked background mutation).

comments.author: Dicklesworthstone
comments.text: Execution update (RedPlateau): using bv robot triage, I took highest-impact bead bd-xzt.2.2 and claimed non-conflicting surface src/tui/test_harness.rs (reservation id 1878) due active conflicts on src/tui/model.rs (SilverHarbor) and src/tui/update.rs/src/tui/runtime.rs (TanBasin). Implemented deterministic replay extensions: HarnessStep scripted inputs, run_script(), command_trace(), and trace_digest() (stable SHA-256 over state/command transitions). Added tests scripted_replay_yields_stable_transition_digest and script_command_trace_exposes_effect_boundaries.
