Global clippy still reports broad pre-existing warnings in unrelated files (notably src/tui/e2e_artifact.rs, src/tui/render.rs, src/tui/widgets.rs, src/tui/theme.rs, src/tui/test_artifact.rs, src/daemon/loop_main.rs).

title: Build typed data adapters for daemon state file, staleness checks, and live filesystem fallback
description:
Background
Dashboard reliability depends on honoring existing state-file contracts and safe degraded behavior when daemon data is stale/unavailable.

Work
- Implement adapter layer for `DaemonState` ingestion and schema-safe mapping into view models.
- Preserve stale-state thresholds and explicit stale/unknown UI states.
- Implement fallback data path via filesystem stats collector when daemon state cannot be trusted.

Acceptance criteria
- Adapter behavior matches baseline semantics for fresh, stale, missing, and malformed state file cases.
- Fallback path is bounded and clearly visible to operators in the UI.
- Adapter layer has unit tests for success/failure permutations.

comments.author: Dicklesworthstone
comments.text: Started execution with typed state-file/fallback adapter validation. Added/verified stale, missing, malformed, and fallback-mount dedupe coverage in src/tui/adapters.rs. Validation: cargo fmt --check passed; rch exec "cargo test --lib --features tui tui::adapters::" passed (7 tests); rch exec "cargo check --all-targets --features tui" passed. Full clippy currently blocked by unrelated concurrent changes in src/tui/widgets.rs and src/tui/test_harness.rs.

title: Add telemetry query adapters for timeline/explainability panes without breaking logger contracts
description:
Background
Advanced screens need richer event context from SQLite/JSONL, but schema and write-path safety must not regress.

Work
- Implement read adapters for activity/decision evidence needed by timeline and explainability screens.
- Keep adapter boundaries read-only and resilient when one backend is unavailable.
- Surface partial-data states clearly when telemetry backends degrade.

Acceptance criteria
- Timeline/explainability adapters return stable typed views from existing logger schemas.
- No changes are required to critical logging write paths to support dashboard reads.
- Adapter errors degrade gracefully with explicit UI indicators.

title: Implement reusable layout and theme foundation inspired by FrankentUI showcase primitives
description:
Background
We want the best parts of frankentui look/structure, but tuned to SBH operator readability and safety use-cases.

Work
- Define shared spacing, color, emphasis, and semantic status tokens (green/yellow/orange/red/critical).
- Implement responsive pane composition primitives for narrow and wide terminals.
- Include accessibility hooks (high contrast, reduced motion, no-color compatibility).

Acceptance criteria
- Screens share one consistent theming/layout vocabulary.
- Visual hierarchy emphasizes critical pressure/safety states first.
- Theming works in color and no-color paths without losing semantic clarity.
close_reason: Completed

comments.author: Dicklesworthstone
comments.text: Attempted to start after bv triage but encountered active reservation conflicts on src/tui/mod.rs, src/tui/render.rs, and src/tui/widgets.rs held by IndigoStream. Deferring edits on this bead to avoid overlap; execution focus moved to bd-xzt.2.3.

title: Build global input system: keymap engine, contextual help, and command palette backbone
description:
Background
A multi-screen cockpit requires coherent keyboard-first interaction, not ad hoc per-screen key handling.

Work
- Define global vs screen-local key handling precedence.
- Implement command palette backbone with discoverable actions and fuzzy command routing.
- Provide contextual help overlays and consistent quit/escape semantics.

Acceptance criteria
- Key conflicts are deterministic and documented.
- Command palette can trigger cross-screen actions without fragile coupling.
- Help overlay reflects real-time bindings and screen context.
close_reason: All acceptance criteria met: input layer in input.rs (resolve_key_event, palette search, contextual help), update.rs integration via apply_input_action, runtime.rs routing via map_key_event. 83 update tests + 12 input tests passing. Commit b2029b4.

comments.author: Dicklesworthstone
comments.text: Progress: input-layer backbone extended with deterministic palette search/ranking API plus tests for precedence/stability/limits. Validation passed via rch (cargo test --lib --features tui tui::input::, cargo check --all-targets, cargo clippy --all-targets -- -D warnings). Waiting on update/runtime reservation handoff to wire resolve_key_event into reducer runtime path without conflicts.

comments.author: Dicklesworthstone
comments.text: Further progress: added route_palette_query(query)->Option<InputAction> plus fuzzy subsequence fallback matcher in input.rs to satisfy command-palette routing requirements. Added 2 tests for direct routing and fuzzy shorthand (e.g., jbal -> JumpBallast). Revalidated with rch check/clippy and tui::input tests; cargo fmt --check now passing.

comments.author: Dicklesworthstone
comments.text: Partial integration step landed in src/tui/runtime.rs: key events now flow through input::map_key_event before update dispatch. This centralizes key-event mapping and sets up full reducer-side keymap dedup once update.rs handoff is available.

title: Implement bounded subscriptions/timers and background refresh scheduler for dashboard data flows
description:
Background
Continuous dashboards can leak resources or thrash CPU if refresh and async work are not governed centrally.

Work
- Implement timer/subscription model for periodic updates and event ingestion.
- Enforce bounded in-flight work and cancellation semantics for stale refresh jobs.
- Respect refresh-floor constraints and avoid starvation across panes/screens.

Acceptance criteria
- Refresh behavior remains stable under slow I/O and high event rates.
- No unbounded queue growth or orphaned background tasks.
- Scheduler behavior is observable for debugging and perf testing.

title: Harden terminal lifecycle and failure handling (raw mode cleanup, panic safety, graceful exit)
description:
Background
Terminal corruption on crashes is unacceptable for operator tooling.

Work
- Ensure robust enter/exit behavior for raw mode and alternate-screen or inline modes.
- Add panic/error guards that always restore terminal state and emit actionable diagnostics.
- Validate quit paths (q/Esc/Ctrl-C) and signal interactions for clean teardown.

Acceptance criteria
- Terminal is restored correctly after normal exit, error exit, and panic exit.
- Failure cases are logged with enough context for debugging.
- Reliability behavior is covered by automated tests where feasible.

title: Implement schema-shielding layer for dashboard data models (state + telemetry)
description:
Background
Dashboard crashes due to schema drift are a common operational failure mode in fast-moving systems.

Work
- Introduce translation/validation layer between raw daemon/log schemas and UI view models.
- Handle missing/new fields gracefully with explicit UI placeholders and warnings.
- Add versioned mapping tests to prevent accidental hard-coupling to transient schema details.

Acceptance criteria
- Minor schema evolution does not hard-fail dashboard startup/rendering.
- Drift is surfaced transparently to operators and maintainers.
- Mapping layer has deterministic error behavior and test coverage.

comments.author: Dicklesworthstone
comments.text:
Execution update (RedPlateau): implemented schema-shielding hardening for state+telemetry adapters.

State-side:
- DashboardSnapshot warning propagation is covered with explicit drift tests (unknown + missing top-level keys) for both fresh and stale state-file paths.

Telemetry-side:
- JsonlTelemetryAdapter now uses a schema-shield parser fallback for legacy/variant JSONL shapes.
- Tracks recovered vs dropped lines and surfaces diagnostics (`jsonl schema-shield recovered=<n> dropped=<n>`).
- Sets `partial=true` when malformed lines are dropped.

Added tests:
- `jsonl_schema_shield_recovers_legacy_alias_fields`
- `jsonl_schema_shield_marks_partial_when_lines_are_dropped`

Validation:
- `cargo fmt` applied
- `rch exec "cargo test --lib --features tui tui::adapters::"` passed (18 tests)
- `rch exec "cargo test --lib --features tui tui::telemetry::"` passed (29 tests)
- `rch exec "cargo check --all-targets --features tui"` passed

title: Track C: Screen suite and operator interactions (overview, timeline, explainability, ballast, diagnostics)
description:
Purpose
Deliver a multi-screen cockpit that copies the strongest practical ideas from /dp/frankentui demo screens while staying aligned with SBH operational workflows.

Scope
- Screen set for overview, action timeline, explainability, scan candidates, ballast operations, and diagnostics.
- Global navigation and interaction ergonomics (keyboard-first command palette + contextual help).
- Accessibility-aware theming and responsive layouts for common terminal sizes.

Why this matters
The major value upgrade comes from richer operator cognition: faster triage, clearer root cause visibility, and less CLI context switching.

## Success Criteria
- Every critical operator workflow is executable in-dashboard with less friction than baseline.
- Screen content and interactions preserve all existing critical visibility while improving decision speed.
- Accessibility and responsive behavior are validated across supported terminal contexts.

comments.author: Dicklesworthstone
comments.text:
UX extraction targets from FrankentUI demos

Prioritize these concrete patterns:
- multi-panel overview hierarchy (`dashboard.rs` style composition),
- bounded severity-filtered event stream (`action_timeline.rs`),
- explainability-first decision drilldown (`explainability_cockpit.rs`),
- command palette guided navigation,
- diagnostics pane for runtime trust.

Avoid demo-only complexity that does not improve incident response (ornamental effects, heavyweight optional modules with weak ops value).

title: Implement Overview screen with full parity plus upgraded situational panels
description:
Background
Overview is the first responder surface and must preserve all current high-signal dashboard information.

Work
- Render pressure state per mount, trend indicators, ballast inventory, and critical counters.
- Preserve/upgrade EWMA sparkline visibility and daemon freshness indicators.
- Add operator-focused hierarchy (critical alerts and bottlenecks surfaced before secondary stats).

Acceptance criteria
- Overview covers all baseline dashboard information with no regressions.
- Critical conditions are visually and textually unmistakable.
- Works in normal and degraded data modes.

title: Run operator workflow benchmark validation (task-time, error-rate, confidence) and feed UX tuning
description:
Background
A better-looking TUI is not enough; we need measurable operator outcomes.

Work
- Define benchmark tasks reflecting real incidents (pressure triage, explainability query, ballast diagnosis, cleanup candidate review).
- Measure baseline vs new cockpit on completion time, error rate, and confidence.
- Convert findings into prioritized UX tuning adjustments before final docs/signoff.

Acceptance criteria
- Benchmark results show equal or improved operator effectiveness vs baseline.
- Regressions are captured as explicit follow-up tasks before go/no-go.
- Findings are documented for future UX iteration cycles.

title: Integrate preference-driven UX controls (default screen, density, hints) into cockpit navigation
description:
## Background
Preference plumbing is only valuable if operator workflows actually consume it. This task applies persistent preferences to real cockpit behavior while preserving high-signal incident visibility.

## Goals
- Apply persisted preferences to startup routing (default screen), panel density, and help/hint verbosity.
- Keep navigation and command palette deterministic regardless of preference profile.
- Provide transparent controls for reset/revert so operators can recover instantly from bad personalization.

## Implementation Scope
- Integrate preference profile into navigation bootstrap and per-screen rendering settings.
- Expose command palette actions for set/reset/revert-to-defaults and indicate active profile mode.
- Enforce safety visibility floor: critical pressure and veto/safety indicators remain visible in all density modes.
- Ensure preference application is idempotent across redraw loops and reload events.

## Test + Logging Requirements
- Unit tests: reducer/state transitions for preference application and reset actions.
- Integration tests: startup default-screen routing, profile persistence round-trip, deterministic command availability.
- E2E scenarios: operator flow comparison with/without preferences, including reset-to-default during active session.
- Logging: structured events for preference apply/reset actions with actor context and resulting profile hash/version.

## Acceptance Criteria
- Preference-driven UX reduces repeated setup friction without hiding critical safety data.
- Operators can always inspect, reset, and override profile behavior in-session.
- Preference UX paths are covered by unit/integration/e2e tests with reproducible logs.
close_reason: Implemented preference action telemetry + profile hash/version logging; preference UX wiring complete

comments.author: Dicklesworthstone
comments.text: Dependency rationale: bd-xzt.3.11 depends on bd-xzt.2.10 and bd-xzt.3.7 to guarantee preference-driven UX is layered on stable preference persistence + finalized navigation semantics. This prevents drift between profile behavior and command-palette/navigation contracts.

title: Implement Action Timeline screen (event stream, severity filters, detail drill-down)
description:
Background
A timeline view improves operator understanding of causality during pressure incidents.

Work
- Build bounded event stream panel with severity/component filters and follow mode.
- Support detail pane for selected events with enough context to map to logs/explain traces.
- Keep deterministic ordering and stable behavior under bursty event ingestion.

Acceptance criteria
- Operators can quickly isolate high-severity events without leaving the dashboard.
- Timeline remains responsive with high event volume.
- Event-to-detail mapping is reliable and testable.
notes: Implemented full Action Timeline screen (S2): SeverityFilter enum, timeline state fields in DashboardModel, cursor/filter/follow methods, TelemetryTimeline msg, TimelinePane/TimelineLayout with narrow/wide builders, render_timeline with filter bar/event list/detail pane/severity badges/status footer, timeline key handlers (j/k/arrows, f filter, F follow), tick telemetry integration. Comprehensive tests across all 4 files. cargo check and clippy pass.

title: Implement Explainability cockpit for decision rationale, veto reasons, and policy state
description:
Background
SBH decisions must be explainable; operators need in-dashboard access to rationale during incidents.

Work
- Build panes for recent decisions, factor contributions, veto reasons, and policy mode transitions.
- Link explain data to concrete artifacts (decision IDs, timestamps, affected paths/components).
- Surface uncertainty/drift indicators where relevant to trust assessment.

Acceptance criteria
- Operators can answer “why did this happen/not happen?” without leaving dashboard context.
- Explainability data never implies stronger certainty than available evidence.
- Failure/partial-data states are explicit, not silent.

title: Implement Scan Candidates screen with score breakdown and safety-veto visibility
description:
Background
Operators need fast inspection of cleanup candidates and why items are included/excluded.

Work
- Display ranked candidates with factor-level score contributions and estimated reclaim size.
- Expose safety-veto and protection reasons (marker, glob, open-file, git, permissions).
- Support sorting/filtering for high-impact triage.

Acceptance criteria
- Candidate list is deterministic and consistent with scanner/scoring outputs.
- Vetoed/protected paths are understandable without digging through raw logs.
- Screen does not bypass existing deletion safety policy.

title: Implement Ballast Operations screen with per-volume inventory and guarded action flow
description:
Background
Ballast release/replenish is central to incident response; operators need clear, safe visibility and actions.

Work
- Display per-volume ballast inventory, release/replenish status, and recent operations.
- If interactive actions are enabled, require explicit confirmation and guard messaging before state-changing commands.
- Make mount-to-ballast mapping obvious to avoid wrong-volume assumptions during incidents.

Acceptance criteria
- Ballast state in dashboard matches CLI/status outputs.
- Action affordances (if present) are safe-by-default and auditable.
- Screen remains useful in read-only mode when actions are disabled by policy.

title: Implement Diagnostics/Performance HUD for frame health, refresh lag, and data-source state
description:
Background
Operators and maintainers need immediate visibility into dashboard health to trust it during long-running sessions.

Work
- Add diagnostics pane for frame timing, skipped frames, refresh lag, and data adapter error counts.
- Surface active mode details (normal/degraded/stale-data) and scheduler pressure.
- Provide lightweight toggles for verbose diagnostics to avoid clutter in normal operation.

Acceptance criteria
- Diagnostics are useful for troubleshooting without requiring external profilers.
- Overhead is low enough to keep normal dashboard performance within budget.
- Metrics definitions are documented and testable.

title: Complete cross-screen navigation model and command palette action catalog
description:
Background
Screen implementations are incomplete until cross-screen navigation and global actions are coherent.

Work
- Implement tab/screen switching, focused-pane navigation, and breadcrumb/context state.
- Register command palette actions for key workflows (jump to pressure hotspots, open explain detail, inspect ballast, search timeline).
- Ensure action routing remains deterministic even during rapid refresh/input.

Acceptance criteria
- Operators can traverse major workflows without memorizing hidden key combos.
- Command palette and direct key bindings remain consistent and conflict-free.
- Navigation state survives refresh cycles without jarring resets.

title: Accessibility and responsive hardening pass across all dashboard screens
description:
Background
A high-density ops TUI must remain usable across terminal sizes and accessibility constraints.

Work
- Validate screen behavior at narrow/medium/wide terminal dimensions with stable fallbacks.
- Ensure high contrast and no-color modes preserve semantic meaning and alert salience.
- Minimize motion/distraction in critical incident scenarios while preserving orientation cues.

Acceptance criteria
- No screen becomes functionally unusable at supported terminal sizes.
- Color is never the only channel conveying critical state.
- Accessibility behavior is documented and covered by tests/snapshots where feasible.

title: Add incident workflow shortcuts (guided actions/playbook links) for high-pressure scenarios
description:
Background
During incidents, operators need minimal-latency navigation to the right actions and evidence views.

Work
- Provide workflow shortcuts from alerts to relevant screens/panes (timeline, explainability, ballast, candidate review).
- Add optional in-dashboard playbook hints/linkouts for recurring incident patterns.
- Ensure shortcuts are keyboard-first and non-intrusive in calm periods.

Acceptance criteria
- Incident triage path length is reduced vs baseline manual navigation.
- Shortcut actions are deterministic and easy to audit.
- Feature can be disabled if operators prefer a minimal dashboard.

title: Track D: Verification matrix (unit, integration, snapshot, e2e, perf) with high-fidelity logging
description:
Purpose
Establish a comprehensive verification harness that proves the new TUI is correct, stable, performant, and operable under real pressure scenarios.

Scope
- Granular unit tests for model/update/render helpers and adapter failure modes.
- Integration tests for command semantics and state-file contracts.
- E2E script expansion with deterministic assertions and rich per-case logs.
- Performance/load tests and quality-gate orchestration via rch.

Why this matters
A visual overhaul without deep tests is high-risk for this safety-critical tool. This track makes regressions cheap to detect and triage.

## Success Criteria
- Unit/integration/snapshot/e2e/stress/parity suites exist and run in reproducible quality gates.
- Interactive behavior is testable via automated harnesses with detailed artifact capture.
- Test logs are structured and actionable enough for rapid failure diagnosis.

comments.author: Dicklesworthstone
comments.text:
Verification philosophy

The old e2e script still contains a dashboard stub expectation; this overhaul is not done until real interactive dashboard behavior is e2e-validated with rich logs.

Test stack expectations:
- fast deterministic unit tests for state/adapters,
- integration tests for command/state contracts,
- snapshot tests for layout regressions,
- e2e smoke + degraded-mode + exit-path checks,
- stress/perf suites for long-session stability.

If a behavior cannot be asserted automatically, document exact manual validation steps and required artifacts.

title: Add unit tests for model reducers, adapters, keymap logic, and render helpers
description:
Background
Reducer-heavy UI architecture is only reliable if state transitions and helper math are unit-tested exhaustively.

Work
- Add table-driven unit tests for model/update transitions and edge cases.
- Add adapter tests for stale/missing/malformed data and fallback semantics.
- Add tests for keymap resolution, command routing, and critical render helper behavior.

Acceptance criteria
- Unit coverage targets high-risk runtime modules introduced by the overhaul.
- Regressions in state transitions and data mapping are caught before integration tests.
- Tests are deterministic and fast enough for routine development loops.

title: Add deterministic replay regression suite using recorded daemon/state/telemetry traces
description:
Background
Replay-based testing catches subtle regressions in state interpretation and rendering decisions with real-world data patterns.

Work
- Define canonical trace fixtures (pressure transitions, stale state, degraded telemetry, ballast events, policy transitions).
- Replay traces through adapters/runtime and assert stable derived view-model/output invariants.
- Add fixture update policy and compatibility checks to avoid silent baseline drift.

Acceptance criteria
- Replay suite detects regressions in data interpretation without requiring live daemon runs.
- Trace fixtures include both normal and failure/degraded scenarios.
- Replay assertions are deterministic across environments.

title: Add property-based tests for reducer invariants, navigation consistency, and scheduler safety
description:
Background
Property-based tests complement examples by discovering edge-case sequences in stateful UI logic.

Work
- Define invariants for reducer state transitions (no invalid focus states, bounded queues, consistent mode transitions).
- Add property-based generators for event streams and input sequences.
- Assert scheduler/backpressure invariants under randomized stress patterns.

Acceptance criteria
- Invariant violations are reproducible with minimized counterexamples.
- Property suite covers critical state-machine and scheduling logic.
- Tests run within practical CI time budgets.

title: Define structured e2e artifact schema (trace IDs, frame dumps, stderr/stdout bundles, timing)
description:
Background
Rich logs are only valuable if consistently structured and easy to correlate across suites and failures.

Work
- Define canonical artifact schema for dashboard tests (trace IDs, command context, timestamps, frame captures, logs, assertions).
- Implement shared helpers in test/e2e scripts to emit schema-compliant artifacts.
- Add validation checks ensuring failing tests always emit minimum diagnostic payloads.

Acceptance criteria
- Every dashboard test failure includes a standardized, machine-readable artifact bundle.
- Artifact naming/retention conventions are documented and enforced.
- Operators can correlate failures across unit/integration/e2e with shared trace IDs.

title: Implement legacy-vs-new dashboard parity harness against frozen contract matrix
description:
Background
To guarantee no feature loss, we need explicit automated parity checks between baseline and overhauled behavior.

Work
- Encode baseline contract matrix from Track A as executable parity assertions.
- Compare key outputs/behaviors across legacy and new dashboard modes under controlled fixtures.
- Flag intentional deltas separately from regressions to keep parity signal honest.

Acceptance criteria
- Parity harness catches contract regressions before release signoff.
- Intentional behavior changes require explicit annotation and approval.
- Harness outputs clear diff artifacts for rapid triage.

title: Add failure-injection test suite for state/log adapter degradations and recovery
description:
## Background
Dashboard reliability must be proven under partial subsystem failure, not assumed. Adapter degradations (state corruption, sqlite/jsonl errors, permissions) are high-risk paths that require explicit failure injection and recovery assertions.

## Goals
- Build deterministic failure-injection coverage for dashboard data adapters and preference persistence boundaries.
- Verify degraded-mode UX communicates data quality/state clearly while preserving core operator controls.
- Verify recovery transitions when faults clear, including state reconciliation and indicator reset behavior.

## Fault Matrix Scope
- Daemon state source: missing file, stale file, malformed JSON, incompatible schema versions.
- Telemetry source: sqlite unavailable/locked, query failure, JSONL append/read failure.
- Preferences source: corrupt preference payload, permission denied, atomic write interruption.
- Environment failures: read-only filesystem paths, transient permission flips, delayed IO surface.

## Test + Logging Requirements
- Unit tests: adapter error mapping, retry/backoff policy, degradation-state reducers.
- Integration tests: mixed-fault scenarios across state + telemetry + preference adapters.
- E2E scripts: fault injection drills with scripted operator recovery actions and expected UI markers.
- Logging artifacts per scenario: scenario_id, injection_point, expected_mode, observed_mode, transition_timestamps, trace_id.

## Acceptance Criteria
- Adapter/runtime degradations are deterministic, operator-visible, and non-panicking.
- Recovery transitions are validated end-to-end (including indicator clear and data freshness recovery).
- Failure-injection suite is part of quality gates and documented in verification runbooks.

comments.author: Dicklesworthstone
comments.text: Dependency rationale: bd-xzt.4.14 depends on state/telemetry/schema/preferences layers (bd-xzt.2.3, bd-xzt.2.4, bd-xzt.2.9, bd-xzt.2.10). This forces fault-injection coverage to span the full adapter surface, including preference corruption and recovery, before runbook and docs signoff.

title: Expand integration tests for dashboard command semantics and state-file contract behavior
description:
Background
Current integration coverage does not adequately protect dashboard runtime behavior.

Work
- Add integration tests for dashboard command execution path and argument semantics.
- Verify stale-state, missing-state, and fallback behavior through realistic fixtures.
- Verify legacy constraints remain enforced (refresh floor, dashboard JSON rejection, safe teardown behavior).

Acceptance criteria
- Integration tests fail on contract regressions that would affect operators.
- Tests are explicit about expected behavior for degraded data conditions.
- Coverage includes both happy-path and critical error-path scenarios.

title: Add snapshot/golden tests for key screens across terminal sizes and color modes
description:
Background
Complex TUI layouts regress subtly; snapshots provide low-friction guardrails for visual structure drift.

Work
- Add snapshot/golden fixtures for overview, timeline, explainability, and ballast screens.
- Cover narrow/medium/wide dimensions and no-color/high-contrast variants where practical.
- Add fixture review guidance to keep snapshots maintainable and meaningful.

Acceptance criteria
- Snapshot diffs make layout regressions obvious and actionable.
- Tests avoid brittle noise by stabilizing dynamic fields where appropriate.
- Snapshot suite runs as part of standard quality gates.

title: Overhaul scripts/e2e_test.sh dashboard section with real interactive coverage and rich case logs
description:
Background
The current e2e dashboard coverage still contains stub-era assumptions; this must be upgraded for the new cockpit.

Work
- Replace dashboard stub checks with real smoke/behavior assertions against the new runtime.
- Add deterministic checks for startup, render loop liveness, key-driven exit, and degraded-mode indicators.
- Ensure per-case logs capture command, exit status, elapsed time, and relevant stdout/stderr artifacts.

Acceptance criteria
- E2E suite validates real dashboard behavior instead of placeholder output.
- Log artifacts are detailed enough to diagnose failures without rerunning immediately.
- E2E remains CI/operator friendly (clear pass/fail summary and artifact locations).

title: Add stress/performance tests for long-running dashboard sessions and bursty telemetry
description:
Background
The dashboard will run for long periods during active agent workloads; perf regressions must be caught proactively.

Work
- Add stress scenarios for high refresh frequency, large event streams, and intermittent adapter failures.
- Measure/assert frame-time, CPU usage envelope, and memory stability against budgets.
- Capture performance traces/log summaries suitable for regression comparison over time.

Acceptance criteria
- Stress suite catches sustained-loop regressions that unit tests miss.
- Performance metrics are compared against explicit thresholds from acceptance gates.
- Results are logged in a format that supports trend tracking.

title: Create quality-gate runbook for rch check/clippy/test + dashboard e2e/perf suites
description:
Background
A massive overhaul needs one authoritative quality-gate sequence for local and CI execution.

Work
- Define ordered gate execution using required remote compilation flow (`rch exec` for CPU-heavy checks).
- Include dashboard unit/integration/snapshot/e2e/stress suites in gate definition.
- Define artifact capture and failure triage expectations for each gate stage.

Acceptance criteria
- Gate sequence is explicit, reproducible, and automation-friendly.
- Failures identify which quality dimension regressed (correctness, UX contract, performance, reliability).
- Gate policy is referenced by rollout and release tasks.

title: Add fallback/rollback verification matrix (legacy path toggle, degraded data, terminal recovery)
description:
Background
Rollout safety depends on proven escape hatches, not assumptions.

Work
- Define and test scenarios for legacy dashboard toggle, degraded telemetry, stale state, and runtime failure recovery.
- Verify rollback actions restore expected operator capabilities without manual surgery.
- Ensure rollback testing includes terminal-state integrity checks.

Acceptance criteria
- Rollback procedures are validated by automated tests or reproducible scripts.
- Safety fallback behavior is exercised under realistic failure triggers.
- Rollback verification is part of final release gates.

title: Add scenario-driven dashboard e2e drills (pressure escalation, ballast response, explainability traceability)
description:
Background
Simple smoke tests are insufficient; we need realistic scenario drills that mirror how operators use SBH under stress.

Work
- Add e2e scenarios that simulate pressure transitions and verify dashboard state transitions.
- Validate ballast-related dashboard signals against expected command/runtime behavior.
- Validate explainability trace linkage from incident event to rationale panes/artifacts.

Acceptance criteria
- Scenario suite catches cross-component regressions (monitor/control/scanner/logging/dashboard integration).
- Drill logs are rich enough for post-mortem use.
- Scenario outcomes are deterministic enough for CI usage or clearly documented if environment-sensitive.

title: Build PTY-driven dashboard interaction harness for automated keyflow tests
description:
Background
Reliable automated testing of full-screen terminal apps requires a PTY harness that can drive input and capture render behavior.

Work
- Build or integrate a PTY harness that launches `sbh dashboard` and injects key sequences deterministically.
- Capture terminal output/frame snapshots with timing metadata suitable for assertions.
- Provide helpers for common flows (startup, navigation, command palette open/close, quit paths).

Acceptance criteria
- Interactive flows can be tested headlessly in CI-like environments.
- Harness captures enough context to debug flaky interaction failures.
- Harness APIs are reusable by smoke and scenario e2e suites.

title: Track E: Rollout, documentation, migration safety, and operator adoption
description:
Purpose
Ship the overhaul safely: clear operator docs, migration toggles, fallback strategy, and release criteria that minimize incident risk.

Scope
- Update README + operator/testing docs for new dashboard workflows.
- Provide rollout gates and rollback path (legacy dashboard switch + kill switch behavior).
- Capture final parity evidence and handoff notes for future maintainers.

Why this matters
Even a technically excellent TUI fails if operators cannot trust rollout behavior or quickly recover during incidents.

## Success Criteria
- Rollout controls, rollback procedures, and go/no-go signoff are evidence-backed and reproducible.
- Operator and maintainer docs match actual runtime/test behavior at release time.
- Post-release monitoring and deprecation policy are explicit and operationally safe.

comments.author: Dicklesworthstone
comments.text:
Rollout posture

Default-switch decision must be evidence-driven and reversible.

Minimum release expectations:
- objective pass on acceptance/perf/error budgets,
- validated fallback and rollback controls,
- operator docs updated before cutover,
- post-release monitoring defined in advance.

Do not accept “we can fix forward” as the primary mitigation for operator-facing regressions.

title: Update README/operator docs with new dashboard workflows, screens, and keybindings
description:
Background
The overhaul changes how operators interact with SBH in real time; docs must match reality at launch.

Work
- Update README dashboard sections with new capabilities and usage patterns.
- Add screen-by-screen operator guidance for core incident workflows.
- Document keybindings, command palette usage, and degraded-mode interpretation.

Acceptance criteria
- Operators can perform core workflows using docs alone.
- Documentation reflects final command/runtime behavior exactly.
- Troubleshooting entries include new dashboard-specific failure modes.

title: Update testing/logging docs for new dashboard verification and artifact expectations
description:
Background
Future maintainers need a single source of truth for how to validate and debug dashboard behavior.

Work
- Update docs/testing-and-logging.md with new unit/integration/snapshot/e2e/perf coverage map.
- Document required log artifacts and naming conventions for failing dashboard tests.
- Include quick triage guidance for common failure classes.

Acceptance criteria
- Test and logging docs align with implemented quality gates.
- A maintainer can run/interpret the dashboard verification stack without tribal knowledge.
- Documentation is explicit about remote compilation requirements for heavy checks.

title: Define and implement phased rollout controls (default switch, legacy fallback flag, kill switch)
description:
Background
A hard cutover without guard rails is unnecessary risk for an operator-critical interface.

Work
- Define phased rollout approach with explicit default-switch criteria.
- Ensure legacy fallback control and emergency kill switch behavior are deterministic and documented.
- Integrate rollout controls with test gates and rollback verification.

Acceptance criteria
- Operators can reliably switch between new and fallback behavior during incidents.
- Control semantics are unambiguous and tested.
- Rollout progression requires objective evidence from verification track.

title: Run final parity + quality signoff and publish go/no-go decision artifact
description:
Background
Before locking in the new dashboard as default, we need a final evidence-based release decision.

Work
- Execute full acceptance and quality-gate matrix.
- Produce parity checklist evidence for baseline contracts and upgraded features.
- Publish go/no-go summary with unresolved risks, mitigations, and explicit owner signoff.

Acceptance criteria
- Go/no-go decision cites concrete evidence, not intuition.
- Any residual risk is documented with an operational mitigation plan.
- Decision artifact is archived where future maintainers can locate it quickly.

title: Define post-rollout monitoring, incident playbook updates, and maintainer handoff package
description:
Background
Sustained reliability requires clear post-release observability and ownership transfer artifacts.

Work
- Define post-rollout monitoring signals and alert thresholds for dashboard health/regressions.
- Update incident playbook snippets to incorporate new cockpit workflows.
- Produce maintainer handoff notes (architecture map, known limitations, next improvements).

Acceptance criteria
- Post-release monitoring can detect and triage dashboard regressions quickly.
- Incident runbooks reflect the new operator surface.
- Handoff package reduces future archaeology time for contributors.

title: Decide legacy dashboard code deprecation path and long-term fallback retention policy

title: [macOS E15] Release engineering (codesign, notarize, brew tap)
description:
# Epic 15: Release engineering for macOS (signed, notarized, distributable)

## What "release-ready" means on Mac

A binary that runs on any modern Mac without:
1. The dreaded "cannot be opened because the developer cannot be verified" Gatekeeper popup.
2. The "Apple cannot check this app for malicious software" warning.
3. The "permission denied" when invoking from non-interactive context.

Required:
- Code signed with an Apple Developer ID Application certificate.
- Notarized via Apple's notarization service (xcrun notarytool).
- Stapled with the notarization ticket (xcrun stapler staple).
- Hardened runtime entitlements correct (`com.apple.security.cs.allow-jit` we don't need; we DO need access to read various dirs which doesn't require entitlements but does require Full Disk Access at runtime).

## Distribution channels (priority order)

1. **GitHub Release tarball** (matches existing pattern). User runs `curl|bash` installer.
2. **Homebrew tap** (`Dicklesworthstone/storage_ballast_helper`, formula auto-updated by release workflow). Most Mac users have brew.
3. **Standalone installer pkg** (out of scope v1, future work).

## Tasks (see child beads)

1. Apple Developer Program enrollment confirmation (Org or individual; operator decides).
2. Apple Developer ID Application certificate creation. Store private key + cert in CI secret store (encrypted, time-limited access).
3. CI release workflow update: build on macos-latest → codesign → notarize → staple → tarball → upload to release. Include SHA256.
4. Ad-hoc signing for CI test runs (no developer account needed, no Gatekeeper but works for testing).
5. arm64 + x86_64 separate tarballs (no fat binary per scope decision).
6. Release artifact naming convention: `sbh-<version>-aarch64-apple-darwin.tar.xz` (matches existing convention).
7. Homebrew tap repo skeleton: `Dicklesworthstone/homebrew-sbh` with auto-updated formula on release.
8. Brew formula: handles install (symlink to `~/.local/bin`), launchctl bootstrap, post-install message about FDA.
9. Notarization: handle async (submit → poll → ticket). Workflow waits up to 30 min; if notarization fails, build fails with diagnostic output.
10. Spot-check workflow on every PR (build only, no notarization since notarization needs prod cert).
11. Self-update path: `sbh update` on macOS downloads, verifies signature/notarization (`spctl -a`), replaces binary, kickstarts daemon.

## Key gotchas

- Gatekeeper applies to executables run from Finder or via `open`, NOT to executables run from terminal. So `~/.local/bin/sbh` in PATH works without notarization. **But** when sbh installer downloads via curl|bash and chmod +x, the quarantine xattr is *not* set (because curl doesn't set it). So curl|bash works without notarization too.
- BUT: when sbh is invoked by launchd, the "downloader" process info matters. If sbh was downloaded via Safari (which DOES set quarantine xattr), launchd refuses to load it without notarization.
- **Decision:** notarize anyway. Cost is low; it future-proofs us.
- Notarization requires Hardened Runtime which requires Apple's specified linker flags. cargo handles this with `[target.aarch64-apple-darwin] rustflags = [...]` pattern.
- macOS sometimes silently strips xattrs on filesystem operations; this can break stapled tickets. Mitigation: always re-staple after install.

## Failure modes

- Cert expires unnoticed → all releases broken until renewal. Mitigation: monitor cert expiration; alert 30 days out.
- Notarization service rejects (rare; usually format issues). Mitigation: comprehensive notarization log capture in CI; surface error promptly.
- Brew formula tap broken silently → users stuck on old version. Mitigation: bump formula in same release workflow that publishes binaries; verify with `brew test` in CI.
- Self-update on Mac races with launchd KeepAlive (binary replaced while running, daemon restarts mid-replace). Mitigation: write to temp file, rename atomically, then kickstart.

title: Add Developer ID CSR handoff to release doctor
description: After Apple Developer Program enrollment is confirmed, sbh doctor --release should expose the non-secret CSR/keychain request step before certificate export/secrets. Add a stable setup_steps entry and docs/static coverage so operators can move from enrollment to Developer ID cert creation without guessing.
close_reason: Added release doctor CSR/keychain request setup step and documented the enrolled Developer ID certificate flow.

title: Limit Developer ID CSR private-key ACL in release doctor
description: The release doctor CSR setup command should use certtool's current-user private-key ACL option so the generated Developer ID request key is not created with broader access than needed. Update docs and static coverage accordingly.
close_reason: Release doctor and macOS guide now use certtool's current-user ACL option when creating the Developer ID CSR keypair.

title: Verify Gatekeeper acceptance before macOS release packaging
description: After notarization accepts a macOS release binary, run spctl Gatekeeper assessment in the release workflow before packaging/uploading the tarball. Document the release-side Gatekeeper check and add static coverage so the install-time trust gate is not the only verification point.
close_reason: Completed: release workflow now runs Gatekeeper spctl assessment after notarization and before packaging, with docs and static coverage.

title: Support App Store Connect API key notarization
description: Replace the app-specific-password-only notarization path with App Store Connect API key secrets so the release workflow, release doctor, and macOS guide match the validated Vault credential material. Update CI release-doctor presence flags and static tests.
close_reason: Implemented App Store Connect API key notarization across release workflow, release doctor, docs, and CI secret presence checks; verified with fmt, YAML parse, remote check/bin tests/lib tests/clippy, and populated GitHub notary secrets from Vault.

title: [E15.3] CI release workflow: build → codesign → notarize → staple → upload
description: CI release workflow update: build on macos-latest → codesign → notarize → staple → tarball → upload to release. Include SHA256SUMS. Workflow trigger: tag push v*.*.*

comments.author: Codex
comments.text: Release notarization/stapling contract review on 2026-05-10: the open bead title still says codesign -> notarize -> staple -> upload, but current repo behavior intentionally does not run stapler for the CLI tarball flow. Apple current notarization guidance says the notary service accepts disk images, signed flat installer packages, and ZIP archives, and that tickets are attached with stapler to an app, bundle, disk image, or flat installer package. The release workflow therefore creates a temporary ZIP around the signed sbh binary for notarytool, polls until Accepted, runs spctl -a -t execute -vv against the signed binary before packaging, and ships the existing sbh-{tag}-{target}.tar.xz contract. docs/macos.md explicitly documents that the current CLI tarball flow does not staple a ticket because stapler-supported distributable containers are app/bundle, disk image, or flat installer package, and says a future pkg/dmg path should staple and validate that package. I found no source patch needed for this review; the remaining bd-ykwh.3 blockers are still live Developer ID/notary credentials and a successful signed/notarized tag release.

comments.text: Release preflight hardening on 2026-05-10 21:39 UTC: release.yml now runs Homebrew Tap Token Preflight before the build matrix by making build depend on [quality-gate, homebrew-tap-token-preflight]. The preflight validates HOMEBREW_TAP_TOKEN against Dicklesworthstone/homebrew-sbh, requires main as default branch, requires write-capable repo permissions, and rejects broad classic OAuth scopes before any release artifacts are built or a GitHub Release can be published. The final homebrew-tap job keeps the same validation before tap checkout as defense in depth. Docs and static workflow coverage were updated. Proof: cargo fmt --check; ruby YAML parse for release.yml; git diff --check; rch cargo test --lib ci_workflow_spot_checks_macos_release_builds_without_notarization; rch cargo test --lib release_workflow_updates_homebrew_tap_by_pr; rch cargo check --all-targets remote exit 0; rch cargo clippy --all-targets -- -D warnings remote exit 0. title: Set restrictive permissions on state.json temp files
description: state.json temp files created during atomic writes don't set restrictive file permissions. On multi-user systems the daemon state could be readable by other users. Fix: set 0o600 permissions on temp files before rename.

title: [macOS E2] Disk capacity, mount discovery, APFS-aware reporting
description:
# Epic 2: Disk capacity, mount discovery, and APFS-aware reporting (macOS)

## Why this matters

Mac disk reporting is genuinely confusing. The 2026-05-03 incident report illustrated this: `df -h /` showed *11 GB used* but only *147 MB free* on a 1.95 TB disk. That's nonsense unless you understand that on APFS, multiple "volumes" (Data, System, Preboot, VM, Update) share a single container's free pool. The `/` mount is the signed read-only system snapshot. The actual user data lives on `/System/Volumes/Data` (`/dev/disk3s5` in this incident). sbh on Mac MUST present a coherent view: "your container is 99% full" not "your boot volume has 11 GB used".

## Mac disk concepts sbh must understand

1. **APFS containers vs volumes.** A physical disk has one or more containers (`disk3` = container, `disk3s1`, `disk3s2`, ... = volumes within container). All volumes share the container's free pool. Free space on volume A is shared with volume B. `df` shows per-volume but the truth is per-container.
2. **The signed system snapshot.** `/` is a read-only signed snapshot of the OS. Modifications happen on Data. The snapshot consumes ~10 GB. Reclaiming this requires SIP-disabled magic and is out of scope for sbh.
3. **Local Time Machine snapshots.** macOS automatically takes hourly local snapshots ("local snapshots") that retain deleted file space. `tmutil listlocalsnapshots /` shows them. Deleting a file does not free space until the snapshot expires (24h) or is explicitly thinned (`tmutil thinlocalsnapshots /`).
4. **Purgeable storage.** APFS reports some space as "purgeable" — system can free it on pressure (caches, optimized iCloud, large files in iCloud not on disk). `df` may show this as used; Finder may not. The truth is via `diskutil apfs list -plist`.
5. **Firmlinks.** macOS uses firmlinks (`/etc/synthetic.conf` + `/usr/share/firmlinks`) to merge `/System/Volumes/Data/Users` into `/Users` and similar. To users it looks like one tree; underneath there are two volumes. Path-based logic must follow firmlinks correctly.
6. **Disposition snapshots.** On Apple Silicon, additional volumes (Update) hold installer payloads. Usually small (~MB) but occasionally GBs during a pending OS update.

## Required sbh behavior

- `sbh status` reports the *container* capacity, not just the boot volume. Falls back to volume-level if the user explicitly asks.
- `sbh status` shows local snapshots and total space they hold (`Reclaimable from local snapshots: X GB`).
- `sbh status` shows purgeable space and explains what it is.
- `sbh scan` understands that work in `/private/tmp/...` lives on the Data volume and contributes to container fill (it's not a separate temp partition like Linux's tmpfs).
- `sbh scan` does not double-count firmlinked paths.
- `sbh check` (pre-build pressure check) returns the *container* free space, not volume free.

## Tasks (see child beads)

1. statfs/getmntinfo FFI bindings (`libc::statfs`, `libc::getmntinfo`).
2. Container vs volume discovery via `DASession`/`DiskArbitration` framework or `diskutil apfs list -plist` parse fallback.
3. APFS local snapshot enumeration (`tmutil listlocalsnapshots`) + parse + integration into `Capacity` reporting.
4. Purgeable storage detection (`URLResourceKey.volumeAvailableCapacityForImportantUsageKey` or `diskutil info`).
5. Firmlink resolution helper (read `/usr/share/firmlinks` + `/etc/synthetic.conf`).
6. `MountInfo` struct populated with: `device`, `mount_point`, `fs_type`, `container_id`, `total_bytes`, `available_bytes`, `purgeable_bytes`, `is_apfs_data_volume`, `is_apfs_system_snapshot`, `is_apfs_vm_volume`.
7. New `Capacity` semantics: `container_total`, `container_available`, `volume_total`, `volume_available`, `purgeable_in_container`, `local_snapshots_in_container`. JSON output adds these fields under `"platform.darwin.apfs"`.
8. Integration test: on a real macOS dev machine, `sbh status --json` must agree with `diskutil apfs list -plist` to within 100 MB on container_total and container_available.

## Reference material

- `man statfs(2)`, `man getmntinfo(3)`, `man tmutil(8)`
- Apple docs: APFS Reference, Disk Arbitration Framework
- `diskutil apfs list -plist` output schema (parse with `plist` crate)
- prior art: Carbon Copy Cloner, OmniDiskSweeper for handling of these concepts

## Failure modes to avoid

- Reporting "Mac is 50% full" when in reality the container is 99% full (because we measured the wrong volume).
- Treating `/private/tmp` as a separate fs (it is not on macOS — it lives on `/System/Volumes/Data`).
- Reclaiming a file but not telling the user that the bytes won't free until a Time Machine snapshot expires.
- Counting purgeable space as "free" when telling user "you have plenty of room" — operator then adds 200 GB and disk fills because purgeable bytes were already kind of being used for caches that the system was reluctant to drop.

## Why this is its own epic

Disk capacity reporting is the *foundation* of everything sbh does. If we get it wrong on Mac, every downstream feature (scan, clean, dashboard, blame) is off. This epic must land cleanly and be heavily tested before E5/E8/E12 build on top of it.
close_reason: Completed APFS capacity epic: all E2 child beads are closed; status and check use APFS container capacity, nested platform.darwin.apfs JSON is present, /private/tmp and cleanup stop checks use MacOsPal fs_stats container semantics, and live macOS/diskutil plus Linux remote gates passed.

title: [E2.1] FFI bindings: statfs(2), getmntinfo(3) for macOS
close_reason: Implemented macOS statfs foundation without adding unsafe code: added src/platform/macos/sys.rs with static libc::statfs layout assertions, safe nix::statfs snapshots, mounted-volume discovery, and unit tests for /tmp/root mount discovery. Wired MacOsPal fs_stats, mount_points, is_ram_backed, capacity, and mounts to the adapter. Verification: macOS-host rch bash cargo test --lib platform::macos::sys; macOS-host sbh --json doctor --pal shows disk probes implemented; macOS-host cargo check/clippy via rch bash; direct remote Linux rch cargo check/clippy all-targets; cargo fmt --check; git diff --check.

title: [E2.2] APFS container vs volume discovery (parse diskutil apfs list -plist)
description:
Implement helper that runs `diskutil apfs list -plist` once at PAL init, parses with the `plist` crate, builds a map: device_id (e.g. /dev/disk3s5) → container_id (e.g. /dev/disk3) + role (Data | System | Preboot | VM | Update | None).

Cache the result with a TTL (5 min) and refresh on volume mount/unmount events (DiskArbitration framework subscription, optional v1.5).

Why diskutil parse vs DiskArbitration framework directly: simpler, no Obj-C bridge needed, well-documented plist schema. DA framework is for live event subscriptions which we can add later.

Acceptance: on the operator's actual Mac, output identifies `/dev/disk3` as the container and correctly maps disk3s1, s2, s4, s5, s6 to their roles per the 2026-05-03 incident report.
close_reason: Implemented macOS APFS diskutil plist inventory and bridged container/role metadata into PAL capacity and mounts. Proof: macOS APFS sys tests passed; macOS doctor --pal passed with capacity/mounts implemented and 0 failures; macOS cargo check --all-targets passed; macOS cargo clippy --all-targets -- -D warnings passed; direct remote Linux cargo check --all-targets passed on vmi1153651; direct remote Linux cargo clippy --all-targets -- -D warnings passed on vmi1227854; cargo fmt --check and git diff --check passed.

title: [E2.3] Container-aware Capacity reporting (sums per container, not per volume)
description:
Cross-platform Capacity struct gets new fields under platform.darwin.apfs:
  container_id: String
  container_total_bytes: u64
  container_available_bytes: u64
  volume_role: String
  shared_volumes: Vec<String>

When sbh status queries capacity for /System/Volumes/Data, it returns the *container* totals (because that's what limits actual writes), with a "primary" flag, and lists sibling volumes.

JSON output backwards-compatible: top-level total/available reflect container; legacy keys preserved.

Acceptance: `sbh status --json` on operator's Mac matches `diskutil apfs list -plist` totals to within 100 MB.
close_reason: Status capacity now uses APFS container totals when available and exposes APFS container/volume metadata.

title: [E2.4] Local Time Machine snapshot enumeration (tmutil listlocalsnapshots)
description:
Run `tmutil listlocalsnapshots /` (and per-volume), parse output, build SnapshotInfo: {date, name, retained_bytes_estimate, mount_path}. Sum into Capacity.local_snapshot_bytes.

Note: macOS doesn't directly expose snapshot byte sizes via tmutil. Estimate via:
  (sum of all snapshots) ≈ (df-reported used) − (du -sx of mounted root)
This is approximate but matches what Disk Utility shows. Document the estimation method in code comments.

For exact: shell out to `diskutil apfs list -plist` and look up snapshot block counts (more precise).

Acceptance: when local snapshots are present, sbh status reports them with a non-zero estimate; warns user with "Reclaim via: sudo tmutil thinlocalsnapshots / 9999999999999999 4".
close_reason: Parsed tmutil local snapshots, estimated retained bytes from APFS inventory, and surfaced status reclaim guidance.

title: [E2.5] Purgeable storage detection
description:
macOS APFS designates some space as 'purgeable' — system can free on pressure (caches, optimized iCloud, large iCloud Drive files not on disk). `df` doesn't show this; Finder kind of does.

Implementation: NSURL resourceValues for URLResourceKey.volumeAvailableCapacityForImportantUsageKey. Fallback: parse `diskutil info -plist` Container Total Capacity − Container Free Space − sum(volume used) ≈ purgeable.

Capacity struct gets purgeable_bytes field. sbh status displays separately from "truly free".

CRITICAL: do NOT count purgeable as free in the "do you have room" check. Purgeable is reluctant-free; counting it lies to the operator. Document this prominently.
close_reason: Implemented macOS purgeable APFS capacity reporting via NSURL important-usage capacity with APFS inventory fallback; status reports it separately and pressure math excludes it.

title: [E2.6] Firmlink resolution helper
description:
Read /usr/share/firmlinks (binary plist) and /etc/synthetic.conf to build a mapping of firmlinked paths.

Used by: scanner (don't double-count when walking /Users which firmlinks to /System/Volumes/Data/Users); blame (resolve PID's path to canonical firmlinked location).

Acceptance: helper returns map; passing /Users/jemanuel resolves correctly to its underlying path; tests cover synthetic firmlinks added to a tmp dir.
close_reason: Implemented firmlink/synthetic path mapping helper with fixture and live macOS coverage.

title: [E2.7] Implement MacOsPal::capacity() and ::mounts() using statfs + container discovery
description:
Wire E2.1 + E2.2 + E2.3 + E2.4 + E2.5 into the PAL methods. `MacOsPal::capacity(mount)` returns full Capacity struct with all platform-specific fields populated. `MacOsPal::mounts()` returns Vec<MountInfo> with role + container_id annotations.

Acceptance: unit tests pass; integration test on real Mac matches diskutil; sbh status --json on Mac contains all expected keys.
close_reason: Implemented APFS-aware MacOsPal capacity and mount inventory, including complete macOS mount discovery, role/container annotations, snapshot metadata propagation, and status JSON verification.

title: [E2.8] Integration test: sbh status --json agrees with diskutil within 100 MB
description:
End-to-end test in CI on macos-latest: run `sbh status --json`, run `diskutil apfs list -plist`, compare container_total + container_available, assert delta < 100 MB.

Why 100 MB tolerance: there's intrinsic timing skew (df/diskutil/sbh sample at slightly different moments), plus rounding in human-readable conversions.

Test failure mode investigation: if delta > 100 MB, dump both outputs to artifact, mark test as flaky-investigating not failing. After 3 consecutive flakes, escalate.
close_reason: Added macOS-only integration test comparing sbh status --json APFS Data container totals and available bytes against diskutil within 100 MiB.
